subsystem: Mutt/NeoMutt IMAP header-cache subsystem. imap_hcache_open() derives an on-disk header-cache filename for a given IMAP mailbox and opens (creating if needed) that cache database via mutt_hcache_open(). It sits between the IMAP protocol layer (which supplies mailbox names taken from server responses) and the local filesystem-backed header cache.
provenance: Identified by the Mutt-specific type and function names: IMAP_DATA, IMAP_MBOX, ciss_url_t, header_cache_t, and the helper calls imap_cachepath(), imap_parse_path(), mutt_account_tourl(), url_ciss_tostring(), mutt_hcache_open(), plus the FREE() macro, the LONG_STRING and U_PATH constants, and the global HeaderCache. These are all part of the Mutt/NeoMutt IMAP client (imap/util.c). The sample tag on line 1, '/* sample: primevul_c_train_2666_vuln */', and the eval filename eval_0039_cwe22.c indicate a path-traversal (CWE-22) case.
trust_boundary: The hostile input is the IMAP mailbox NAME. In the else branch it comes from idata->ctx->path parsed by imap_parse_path() into mx.mbox — the mailbox path originates from IMAP server responses (e.g. LIST/LSUB/SELECT) and from remotely-influenced folder state, so a malicious or compromised IMAP server controls its bytes. That name is turned into a filesystem path used to create/open a file. Any '/', '..', or absolute-path content the server smuggles into the mailbox name crosses the network-to-filesystem trust boundary. The caller-supplied 'path' argument is comparatively trusted (local), but still flows into the same filename construction.

what_the_code_does.code: header_cache_t* imap_hcache_open (IMAP_DATA* idata, const char* path)
what_the_code_does.explanation: Function signature. imap_hcache_open takes an IMAP_DATA* idata (the per-connection IMAP session state, which carries the account, connection, and current mailbox context) and a const char* path naming a specific mailbox whose header cache is wanted. When path is NULL the function falls back to the currently selected mailbox in idata->ctx. It returns a header_cache_t* handle to an opened header-cache database, or NULL on failure. This is the entry point that maps an IMAP mailbox to its local cache file.

Local declarations. mx is an IMAP_MBOX struct that imap_parse_path() will populate (its mx.mbox member holds the heap-allocated mailbox name). url is a ciss_url_t URL struct used to render a canonical cache key string. cachepath[LONG_STRING] and mbox[LONG_STRING] are two fixed-size stack buffers; LONG_STRING is Mutt's standard 256-byte size constant. mbox will hold the encoded mailbox path fragment; cachepath will hold the final path/key string passed to the header cache. Both buffers are uninitialized here.

If the caller passed an explicit mailbox path, imap_cachepath() transforms that path into a cache-path fragment and writes it into the mbox buffer, bounded by sizeof(mbox) (256). imap_cachepath applies Mutt's IMAP delimiter handling (typically replacing the server's hierarchy delimiter and prefixing/normalizing) to produce the string that identifies this mailbox in the cache namespace. The size argument means the write into mbox is length-bounded (truncation, not overflow).

If no explicit path was given, the function uses the currently open mailbox. It first guards that idata->ctx is non-NULL, then calls imap_parse_path() to parse idata->ctx->path (the current folder's URL/path string) into the mx structure; a negative return means the path could not be parsed and the function bails out with NULL. On success mx.mbox holds the freshly parsed, heap-allocated mailbox name. imap_cachepath() then converts mx.mbox into the mbox buffer exactly as in the path branch, again bounded by sizeof(mbox). FREE(&mx.mbox) releases the allocated name and NULLs the pointer (Mutt's FREE macro takes the address and zeroes it). Critically, mx.mbox derives from server-influenced folder state.

mutt_account_tourl() fills the url struct from the connection's account (host, user, port, scheme) so the cache key is namespaced per account. url.path is then set to point at the mbox buffer produced above — i.e. the (encoded) mailbox name becomes the path component of the URL. url_ciss_tostring() serializes the url back into a single string in cachepath, bounded by sizeof(cachepath) (256); the U_PATH flag requests inclusion of the path component. The resulting cachepath string is the canonical, per-account cache identifier for this mailbox, and it embeds the mbox fragment verbatim.

Finally the code opens the header cache. HeaderCache is the configured global cache directory/base. cachepath is the per-mailbox key string just built. imap_hcache_namer is a callback that turns (folder, cache-dir) into the concrete on-disk filename. mutt_hcache_open() combines these to locate/create the actual cache file on the local filesystem and returns its handle. Because cachepath carries the server-derived mailbox name, whatever filesystem path this ultimately resolves to is influenced by attacker-controllable bytes.

what_could_go_wrong.explanation: PATH TRAVERSAL (primary). mx.mbox is the mailbox name parsed from idata->ctx->path, which is populated from IMAP server responses. A malicious or compromised IMAP server can name a mailbox with directory-traversal or absolute-path content — e.g. containing '../../../../' sequences or a leading '/'. imap_cachepath() applies IMAP delimiter substitution but is NOT a security sanitizer: it does not neutralize '..' path components or path separators for the purpose of confining the result to the cache directory. That tainted fragment lands in mbox, becomes url.path (line 21), is serialized into cachepath (line 22), and is handed to mutt_hcache_open() (line 24), which creates/opens a file at the resulting location. The impact is that the attacker steers a file create/open (and header-cache writes) to an arbitrary path outside HeaderCache — potential file creation/overwrite and cache poisoning. The invariant the implementation must uphold: the mailbox-derived component must be escaped/encoded (e.g. via mutt_encode_path or equivalent) so that no server-controlled bytes can introduce '/', '..', or absolute-path semantics into the final filesystem path — every component must stay confined under HeaderCache.

SECONDARY TRAVERSAL SURFACE. The same unsanitized flow applies to the caller-supplied 'path' argument: it passes straight through imap_cachepath() into mbox and on to the cache filename with no traversal filtering. Although 'path' is nominally more trusted than server data, in Mutt mailbox strings often originate from remote LIST/LSUB results too, so a caller that forwards a server-provided mailbox name reaches the same file-create primitive. The same encoding invariant is required on this branch; a fix must sanitize the mailbox component regardless of whether it came from the argument or from mx.mbox.

NULL-DEREFERENCE (latent, low). The else branch guards idata->ctx, but nothing here guards idata->conn. mutt_account_tourl dereferences &idata->conn->account, and line 10/16 already dereference idata. If imap_hcache_open is ever reached with idata->conn == NULL (or idata itself NULL), this crashes. In normal flow a header-cache open implies an established connection, so this is a defensive/robustness concern rather than a directly attacker-triggered bug, but the contract that idata and idata->conn are non-NULL is implicit and unchecked.

NO OVERFLOW, BUT TRUNCATION. Both fixed 256-byte buffers are only ever written through size-bounded calls: imap_cachepath(..., sizeof(mbox)) and url_ciss_tostring(..., sizeof(cachepath), ...). So there is no stack buffer overflow here (assuming those helpers honor their size argument). The residual risk is silent truncation: an over-long server mailbox name truncated inside mbox/cachepath could collide two distinct mailboxes onto one cache file (cache confusion / stale-header serving), a correctness/integrity issue rather than memory corruption.

summary: imap_hcache_open() builds a local header-cache filename from an IMAP mailbox name that originates from server-controlled data (mx.mbox parsed from idata->ctx->path) and passes it, without traversal sanitization, into mutt_hcache_open() which creates/opens a file. A malicious IMAP server can embed '../' or absolute-path sequences in a mailbox name to steer that file operation outside the header-cache directory (CWE-22 path traversal). The two 256-byte buffers are size-bounded so there is no overflow; the sole real memory-safety notes are a latent idata->conn NULL-deref and potential cache-key truncation collisions.

reviewed_anchors.anchor: imap_hcache_open(IMAP_DATA* idata, const char* path) signature
reviewed_anchors.reason: Both parameters feed unsanitized mailbox names into a filesystem path construction (traversal surface).
Opaque session pointer; its only security-relevant use is as source of mx.mbox and conn, covered by other anchors.
Flows into imap_cachepath()->mbox->cachepath->file open with no traversal filtering (secondary CWE-22 surface).
Server-derived mailbox name; the primary attacker-controlled path component that reaches mutt_hcache_open().
Local URL builder; carries the tainted mbox as url.path but adds no separate contract beyond the traversal already flagged.
Fixed-size destination buffer paired with a size argument; no overflow but truncation-collision risk noted.
Fixed-size destination buffer written via size-bounded call; carries the tainted mailbox fragment; truncation risk.
Standard Mutt 256-byte size constant; used correctly as the bound for both writes, no contract violation.
Performs IMAP delimiter mapping but does not sanitize '..'/'/' for filesystem confinement — the core traversal enabler.
Return value is checked (<0 -> return NULL); it is the source of tainted mx.mbox but the taint is attributed to mx.mbox anchor.
Correct defensive check; prevents NULL deref of idata->ctx before imap_parse_path.
Frees and NULLs the parsed name once, only in the branch that allocated it; no double-free or leak.
Dereferences idata->conn with no NULL guard (latent CWE-476).
Aliases the tainted mbox into the URL path; taint already tracked under the traversal finding.
Serializer is size-bounded; propagates but does not create the traversal; U_PATH just selects path inclusion.
Flag selecting URL path serialization; no security contract of its own.
The sink: creates/opens a file at the attacker-influenced cachepath; where the traversal actually lands.
Trusted local configuration base directory; not attacker-controlled.
Naming callback; final filename assembler in the impl (not shown) — confirmation target, not a distinct anchor here.

findings.title: Path traversal via server-controlled IMAP mailbox name into header-cache filename
findings.anchor: imap_cachepath (idata, mx.mbox, mbox, sizeof (mbox)) -> url.path = mbox -> mutt_hcache_open(HeaderCache, cachepath, ...)
findings.bug_class: Path traversal / external control of filename (unsanitized remote mailbox name into filesystem path)
findings.invariant: Every mailbox-derived component embedded in the header-cache filename must be escaped/encoded so that no server-controlled bytes can introduce path separators, '..' components, or absolute-path semantics; the final resolved file must be provably confined under the HeaderCache directory.
findings.failure_mode: A malicious or compromised IMAP server advertises a mailbox whose name contains directory-traversal sequences (e.g. '../../../../home/user/.ssh/authorized_keys' or a leading '/'). imap_parse_path() places it in mx.mbox; imap_cachepath() maps delimiters but does not neutralize '..'/'/' for filesystem confinement; the tainted fragment becomes url.path, is serialized into cachepath, and mutt_hcache_open() creates/opens a header-cache file at the resulting location. Result: attacker-steered file creation/overwrite and header-cache writes outside the cache directory, i.e. arbitrary-path write/poisoning triggered purely by connecting to the hostile server.
findings.what_to_confirm: Confirm whether imap_cachepath() (or a later step such as mutt_encode_path / imap_hcache_namer) actually strips or percent-encodes '/', '..', and leading separators from the mailbox name before it reaches mutt_hcache_open(); if not, the traversal is exploitable. Check the fixed Mutt/NeoMutt version to see the added mutt_encode_path() sanitization.

findings.title: Caller-supplied 'path' argument reaches the same file-create sink without sanitization
findings.bug_class: Path traversal (secondary input path for external control of filename)
findings.invariant: The mailbox component must be sanitized regardless of source; the 'path' branch must apply the same confinement encoding as the mx.mbox branch before the value reaches mutt_hcache_open().
findings.failure_mode: When a caller forwards a mailbox name that itself came from remote LIST/LSUB output into the 'path' parameter, it flows unsanitized through imap_cachepath() into mbox/cachepath and into the same mutt_hcache_open() file operation, yielding the identical traversal primitive via a second code path.
findings.what_to_confirm: Trace all callers of imap_hcache_open() that pass a non-NULL path to determine whether any of them forward server-derived mailbox strings, and confirm the sanitization fix covers this branch as well as the mx.mbox branch.

findings.title: Unchecked idata->conn dereference (latent NULL pointer)
findings.invariant: idata and idata->conn must be non-NULL whenever imap_hcache_open is invoked; if that cannot be guaranteed by all callers, the function must guard them.
findings.failure_mode: The code guards idata->ctx (line 13) but never guards idata->conn before taking &idata->conn->account. If reached with idata->conn == NULL (or idata NULL), it dereferences a NULL/invalid pointer and crashes. Not directly attacker-triggered under normal connected flow, but an unenforced precondition.
findings.what_to_confirm: Verify every caller establishes idata->conn before calling imap_hcache_open, or add an explicit NULL guard.

audit_checklist: Confirm imap_cachepath()/imap_hcache_namer sanitizes '/', '..', and leading '/' from the mailbox name (look for mutt_encode_path or equivalent) so the cache file stays under HeaderCache.
Reproduce with a hostile IMAP server returning a mailbox name containing '../' sequences and observe where mutt_hcache_open() actually creates the file.
Verify both branches (caller 'path' and parsed mx.mbox) route through identical sanitization before the sink.
Confirm url_ciss_tostring() and imap_cachepath() strictly honor their size arguments (no overflow) and characterize truncation-collision behavior between distinct long mailbox names.
Add/verify NULL guards for idata and idata->conn before mutt_account_tourl().
Check that mx.mbox is always freed exactly once (FREE at line 17 only runs in the else branch that allocated it) with no leak on early return paths.
Cross-reference the fixed Mutt/NeoMutt commit history for the IMAP header-cache path-traversal hardening to match the exact sanitization applied.
